brief.name: RE-1 — boutique off-market acquisitions team (LA)  // JOHNNY-EDITABLE name/handle
brief.what_they_do: Founder-led team (2-5) doing off-market / distressed acquisitions in predetermined key areas of LA. Just completed a major project cycle and closed 14 properties in the last 12 months. Researches owners manually (title runs, BeenVerified-style lookups, driving-for-dollars). Holds a raw ~10k-row county data CSV. No CRM or automation tooling. Wants to expand to other cities.

signals.detail: we finally sold everything on it. So right now I have like no houses
The way that we've typically found distressed properties is straight up just driving
Our email thing has been like consistently 30% wrong
fifteen thousand dollars and it's like 40 enriched... this is just way too much so... we'll stick doing it manually
either we need to hire someone else on the team or i could get... like an assistant... the goal is is to start moving to other cities

i'm johnny — an independent AI-automations marketer. i build the data+story layer for sales teams: scraping, enrichment, CRM-from-scratch, agent systems. receipts: built Vortex for a marketing agency on a signed $5,040 two-month contract; my LinkedIn outreach pulled ~40% reply rates vs 2% cold email. the system replaces the manual research hire.
PERSON SCRAPE DEGRADED: person stage skipped: no person inputs on the run (linkedinUrl/xHandle/name all absent)
Real-estate investor client agreed $4-5k flat + 10-20% commission ('anywhere from 4 5 K would be sick')
Marketing agency signed fixed $5,040 / 2 months / 4 modules + ~$250/mo running costs
'name + linkedin' Google-search hack: ~85% hit rate where scrapers get accounts flagged
LinkedIn outreach ~40% reply rate vs cold email ~2% ('if you're doing like good')
The pricing anchor to beat: refused vendor at '$15,000 and it's like 40 enriched'

brief.brief: Carlos-shaped primary: pipeline gap + manual stack + 30%-wrong data + refused $15k vendor + city-expansion ambition. The system replaces the hire.
brief.provenance: fixture · authored verbatim from the Carlos discovery-call corpus (guided, not faked)

event.story:
THE GOAL:
Close the pipeline gap fast and scale the off-market acquisitions model to new cities — Carlos's own words: 'the goal is to start moving to other cities.' The team just cleared its last property and is sitting on a raw ~10k-row county CSV with no active deals in the hopper [pipeline_gap]. The ambition is city-by-city expansion without proportionally growing headcount.

THE OBSTACLE:
The data layer is broken at the foundation. Emails out of that county CSV have been 'consistently 30% wrong' [data_pain] — meaning roughly 3,000 of those 10k rows are dead on arrival before a single outreach goes out. On top of that, the research method is 'straight up just driving' [manual_research]: boots-on-ground, one property at a time, a process that physically cannot scale to a second city without a new hire. That hire is the only expansion path Carlos has priced so far [expansion_intent].

THE OLD WAY FAILS:
The manual-research-plus-driving stack closed 14 properties in LA in 12 months — a real result — but it required the team's own time and geography. The moment the target is a city they can't drive, the model breaks. And the vendor market Carlos already tested offered '$15,000 and it's like 40 enriched' [vendor_refusal] — he walked away and said they'd 'stick doing it manually.' So the two live options — hire a person or pay a vendor — are either too slow or too expensive for what they actually need: a durable, rerunnable enrichment system that turns a raw CSV into a prioritized, story-tagged contact list they can outreach from anywhere.

THE BETTER PATH:
Johnny builds the data-and-story layer Carlos doesn't have: scrape, enrich, and score the existing ~10k-row county CSV so the 30%-wrong email problem gets cut before a single outreach fires [data_pain / seller_pack]. The 'name + LinkedIn' Google-search hack hits ~85% where scrapers get accounts flagged [seller_proof] — that's the enrichment method, not a black-box vendor charging $15k for 40 rows [vendor_refusal]. The output is a CRM-style system that is rerunnable when Carlos moves to city two or city three, not a one-time data dump that needs to be rebuilt from scratch. Pricing is structured the way Carlos already said felt right: flat base in the $4–5k range plus commission tied to closed deals [seller_proof] — the system pays for itself on one acquisition. The outreach angle, in Johnny's voice: 'You've got 10,000 rows and a proven close rate — the only thing between you and the next city is knowing which 200 rows to call first.'

THE BETTER OUTCOME:
Carlos's team enters the next market with a pre-built, pre-enriched contact list — not a spreadsheet full of bad emails and no phone numbers. The pipeline gap that exists right now [pipeline_gap] becomes a planning window instead of dead time: the 10k-row CSV gets cleaned, scored, and story-tagged while the team is between deals, so day one in a new city looks like day 300 in LA. The manual-research hire never gets made. The $15k vendor never gets another call. And the system reruns on the next county's data without rebuilding from zero — Carlos is in a position to expand on his own terms, city by city, with the data layer already under him.

score.failReason: fabricatedClaims contains unmatched factual claims, including '14 properties' and '12 months'

event.reason: fabricatedClaims contains unmatched factual claims, including '14 properties' and '12 months'

event.story:
THE GOAL:
Close the pipeline gap that opened when the last cycle sold through — and stop rebuilding the same manual stack every time the team eyes a new city. The ambition, in the founder's own framing: "the goal is to start moving to other cities." [expansion_intent]

THE OBSTACLE:
The team is running on a raw ~10k-row county CSV, driving-for-dollars recon, and BeenVerified-style lookups — and the contact data underneath it all is "consistently 30% wrong." [data_pain] That error rate isn't a rounding problem; it's the reason outreach stalls before it starts. Every bad email is a distressed owner who never heard from them, and right now there are no houses in the pipeline. [pipeline_gap]

THE OLD WAY FAILS:
When the data pain got bad enough, the team priced a vendor fix — and got quoted $15,000 for 40 enriched records. [vendor_refusal] The math didn't work, so the decision was to "stick doing it manually." [vendor_refusal] Manual works in one neighborhood you can drive. It doesn't scale to a second city, and it can't fix a 30%-wrong email list without a system underneath it. Hiring an assistant buys bodies, not infrastructure — and the expansion clock is already running. [expansion_intent]

THE BETTER PATH:
Johnny builds the data-and-story layer the team is missing: scraping, enrichment, and a durable CRM-style system rerunnable across any new market — not automation slop that needs to be rebuilt every two months. [seller_positioning] The name-plus-LinkedIn Google-search method hits ~85% where scrapers get accounts flagged, so the contact layer that comes out is actually usable. [seller_proof] The pricing model is a flat base plus commission — the real-estate investor comp structure landed at $4–5k flat plus 10–20% commission, built around the same distressed-acquisition use case. [seller_proof] The $15k vendor quoted 40 enriched records; this system runs the whole 10k-row CSV and reruns it when the team moves markets. [vendor_refusal, seller_proof]
Opening angle: "You're sitting on 10,000 rows and a 30%-wrong email list — before you hire anyone or pick the next city, let's turn that CSV into something you can actually outreach from."

THE BETTER OUTCOME:
The team enters the next market with a clean, enriched contact system already in place — not a driving-for-dollars cold start. The pipeline gap that exists right now [pipeline_gap] becomes the window to build the infrastructure once, so the second city launch doesn't repeat the same manual rebuild. The founder gets to make the expansion decision from a position of data clarity, not data debt — and the system travels with the team wherever they go next. [expansion_intent]

score.failReason: fabricatedClaims contains ungrounded quantities: '10k-row county CSV' and '10,000 rows' do not appear in any Signal

event.reason: fabricatedClaims contains ungrounded quantities: '10k-row county CSV' and '10,000 rows' do not appear in any Signal

event.story:
THE GOAL:
Carlos's team wants to stop working the same LA zip codes and start moving into other cities — scaling a founder-led off-market acquisitions operation beyond its current geography without proportionally scaling headcount. In his own words: 'the goal is to start moving to other cities.' [expansion_intent]

THE OBSTACLE:
Right now the pipeline is empty. Carlos said it plainly: 'we finally sold everything on it. So right now I have like no houses.' [pipeline_gap] The team refills that pipeline the hard way — 'straight up just driving' neighborhoods, running title lookups by hand, doing BeenVerified-style owner research one record at a time. [manual_research] And when they finally get to outreach, roughly 30% of their email data is wrong before the first message goes out. [data_pain] You can't scale to a new city on a stack that breaks one in three times in your home market.

THE OLD WAY FAILS:
Carlos already looked at buying his way out of the manual grind. A vendor quoted him $15,000 for roughly 40 enriched records — his reaction: 'this is just way too much so… we'll stick doing it manually.' [vendor_refusal] So the fallback is hiring: 'either we need to hire someone else on the team or I could get… like an assistant.' [expansion_intent] But a hire doesn't fix the 30%-wrong data problem [data_pain] — it just adds payroll to a broken research process. And a human assistant can't be rerun on a new city's county file the week you decide to expand.

THE BETTER PATH:
Johnny builds the data-and-story layer that replaces the manual research hire — scraping, enrichment, and a durable CRM-style system rerunnable on any new market's county file, not automation slop that needs to be rebuilt every two months. [seller_positioning] A comparable real-estate investor client landed at a $4–5k flat plus 10–20% commission structure — a fraction of the $15,000 quote Carlos already refused for 40 records. [seller_proof] The 'name + LinkedIn' Google-search approach hits roughly 85% of records without getting accounts flagged the way scrapers do [seller_proof], which directly attacks the 30%-wrong email problem. [data_pain] LinkedIn outreach on clean, enriched data pulls ~40% reply rates versus the ~2% cold email baseline [seller_proof] — meaning the same county file produces a fundamentally different conversation rate once the data quality is fixed.
Opening angle, in Johnny's voice: 'You've got a county file sitting there and a new city on the whiteboard — the question is whether you want to drive every street in that city too, or build the system once and point it anywhere.'

THE BETTER OUTCOME:
Carlos's team gets a rerunnable enrichment system they own: clean owner contact data pulled from the county file they already hold, stories layered on each record so outreach reflects the actual situation (tax pressure, absentee signal, forced-sale window), and a CRM scaffold that travels to the next city without a new hire. The 30%-wrong email rate becomes a solvable data problem, not a permanent tax on every campaign. [data_pain] When the expansion city goes live, the research infrastructure is already there — Carlos is in a powerful position because he can plan the move before the pipeline gap forces a scramble.
